nodes.name: Constitutional Amendments
nodes.description: All 27 amendments to the U.S. Constitution

nodes.name: Major U.S. Wars
nodes.description: American military conflicts from the Revolution to the present

config.prompt: Name all 27 Constitutional Amendments by their primary subject, in order

items.answer: Freedom of religion, speech, press, assembly, and petition
items.explanation:
Ratified December 15, 1791 — part of the Bill of Rights (Amendments 1–10)
Prohibits Congress from making laws respecting an establishment of religion, or abridging freedom of speech, press, assembly, or petition
One of the most litigated amendments; key cases include Brandenburg v. Ohio, Tinker v. Des Moines, and New York Times v. Sullivan
The Establishment Clause and Free Exercise Clause together govern church-state separation
items.cardBack: Freedom of religion, speech, press, assembly, and petition

alternates: Right to keep and bear arms
items.explanation:
Ratified December 15, 1791 — part of the Bill of Rights
'A well regulated Militia, being necessary to the security of a free State, the right of the people to keep and bear Arms, shall not be infringed'
District of Columbia v. Heller (2008) established the individual right to bear arms, independent of militia service
McDonald v. City of Chicago (2010) extended this right against state and local governments

Ratified December 15, 1791 — part of the Bill of Rights
Prohibits the government from forcing citizens to house soldiers in peacetime without consent, or in wartime except as prescribed by law
Direct response to the British Quartering Acts of 1765 and 1774, which forced colonists to house British troops
Least litigated of all amendments; has never been the primary basis for a Supreme Court ruling

Ratified December 15, 1791 — part of the Bill of Rights
Protects against unreasonable searches and seizures; requires warrants to be issued only upon probable cause
Mapp v. Ohio (1961) applied the exclusionary rule to states — evidence from illegal searches is inadmissible
Response to British use of 'writs of assistance,' allowing officials to search homes without cause

items.answer: Due process; no self-incrimination or double jeopardy
items.explanation:
Ratified December 15, 1791 — part of the Bill of Rights
Guarantees grand jury indictment for serious crimes, prohibits double jeopardy and self-incrimination, requires due process, and restricts government taking of private property
'Taking the Fifth' refers to invoking the right against self-incrimination
The 'Takings Clause' requires 'just compensation' when the government seizes private property
items.cardBack: Due process; no self-incrimination or double jeopardy

items.answer: Right to speedy trial, jury, and legal counsel
items.explanation:
Ratified December 15, 1791 — part of the Bill of Rights
Guarantees the right to a speedy and public trial by an impartial jury, to be informed of charges, to confront witnesses, and to have an attorney
Gideon v. Wainwright (1963) held that states must provide counsel to defendants who cannot afford it
Miranda v. Arizona (1966) built on this, requiring police to inform suspects of their right to an attorney
items.cardBack: Right to speedy trial, jury, and legal counsel

items.answer: Right to jury trial in civil cases
alternates: Right to jury in civil cases
items.explanation:
Ratified December 15, 1791 — part of the Bill of Rights
Guarantees the right to a jury trial in federal civil cases where the value in controversy exceeds twenty dollars
The twenty-dollar threshold was set in 1791 and has never been adjusted for inflation
One of the least-changed amendments; applies only to federal courts, not state courts
items.cardBack: Right to jury trial in civil cases

items.answer: No cruel and unusual punishment or excessive bail
items.explanation:
Ratified December 15, 1791 — part of the Bill of Rights
Prohibits excessive bail, excessive fines, and cruel and unusual punishment
Derived almost verbatim from the English Bill of Rights (1689)
The Supreme Court has ruled the death penalty is not per se cruel and unusual, but certain applications (e.g., for juveniles, Roper v. Simmons, 2005) violate it
Standard evolves with 'evolving standards of decency' — what was acceptable in 1791 may not be today
items.cardBack: No cruel and unusual punishment or excessive bail

items.explanation:
Ratified December 15, 1791 — part of the Bill of Rights
States that the enumeration of certain rights in the Constitution shall not be construed to deny or disparage other rights retained by the people
Addresses the fear that listing specific rights would imply the government could violate any rights not listed
Justice Goldberg invoked it in Griswold v. Connecticut (1965) to recognize a right to privacy
The least-cited of the Bill of Rights amendments in Supreme Court majorities

items.answer: Powers reserved to states and the people
items.explanation:
Ratified December 15, 1791 — part of the Bill of Rights
States that powers not delegated to the federal government, nor prohibited to the states, are reserved to the states or the people
Foundation of federalism; frequently invoked in disputes between federal and state authority
Interpretation has shifted throughout history — narrowly construed during New Deal expansion; invoked by states challenging federal mandates
Completes the Bill of Rights (Amendments 1–10), all ratified together December 15, 1791
items.cardBack: Powers reserved to states and the people

items.answer: Sovereign immunity; states cannot be sued by citizens of other states
alternates: Federal courts cannot hear suits against states
States cannot be sued by citizens of other states
items.explanation:
Ratified February 7, 1795
Prohibits federal courts from hearing lawsuits brought by citizens of one state against another state
Passed in direct response to Chisholm v. Georgia (1793), where the Supreme Court held Georgia could be sued by a South Carolina citizen
Constitutional amendment overriding a Supreme Court decision — a rare occurrence
Does not prevent states from suing each other or the federal government from suing a state
items.cardBack: Sovereign immunity; states cannot be sued by citizens of other states

items.answer: Separate Electoral College ballots for President and Vice President
alternates: Separate ballots for president and vice president
items.explanation:
Ratified June 15, 1804
Requires electors to cast separate ballots for President and Vice President
Passed after the election of 1800, when Jefferson and his running mate Burr tied in the Electoral College (both received 73 votes), throwing the election to the House
The original system had electors cast two votes for president; the runner-up became VP — leading to politically mismatched administrations
Also addresses what happens if no candidate wins a majority: the House chooses the President, the Senate chooses the VP
items.cardBack: Separate Electoral College ballots for President and Vice President

alternates: Abolition of slavery and involuntary servitude
items.explanation:
Ratified December 6, 1865
Abolishes slavery and involuntary servitude, except as punishment for a crime
First of the three Reconstruction Amendments (13th, 14th, 15th), passed after the Civil War
Lincoln's Emancipation Proclamation (1863) only freed enslaved people in Confederate states; the 13th Amendment abolished slavery nationwide
Georgia was the 27th state to ratify, providing the required three-fourths majority
Mississippi didn't formally ratify until 1995 (and didn't file the paperwork until 2013)

items.answer: Citizenship, equal protection, and due process applies to states
items.explanation:
Ratified July 9, 1868
Grants citizenship to all persons born or naturalized in the U.S. (birthright citizenship); prohibits states from denying due process or equal protection
Second of the three Reconstruction Amendments; directly overturned Dred Scott v. Sandford (1857)
The Equal Protection Clause is the basis for landmark civil rights rulings: Brown v. Board (1954), Loving v. Virginia (1967), Obergefell v. Hodges (2015)
The Due Process Clause has been used to incorporate most Bill of Rights protections against state governments
Section 3 disqualifies from office anyone who engaged in insurrection against the Constitution
items.cardBack: Citizenship, equal protection, and due process applies to states

items.answer: Voting rights cannot be denied on account of race
alternates: Right to vote regardless of race
items.explanation:
Ratified February 3, 1870
Prohibits denying or abridging the right to vote on account of race, color, or previous condition of servitude
Third of the three Reconstruction Amendments; intended to secure voting rights for formerly enslaved men
In practice, Southern states used poll taxes, literacy tests, grandfather clauses, and violence to disenfranchise Black voters for nearly a century
The Voting Rights Act of 1965 was needed to enforce these protections in practice
Women were not included; women's suffrage came 50 years later with the 19th Amendment (1920)
items.cardBack: Voting rights cannot be denied on account of race

items.explanation:
Ratified February 3, 1913
Gives Congress the power to levy and collect an income tax without apportioning it among the states
Passed after the Supreme Court struck down the Income Tax Act of 1894 in Pollock v. Farmers' Loan (1895)
First of four amendments ratified during the Progressive Era (1913–1920)
The modern federal income tax system flows directly from this amendment

items.answer: Direct popular election of U.S. senators
items.explanation:
Ratified April 8, 1913
Requires U.S. senators to be elected by popular vote rather than chosen by state legislatures
Original Constitution (Article I, Section 3) had state legislatures choose senators — this led to corruption and frequent deadlocks
Second of four Progressive Era amendments
Some conservatives have advocated repealing this amendment to restore states' power over Senate composition
items.cardBack: Direct popular election of U.S. senators

items.explanation:
Ratified January 16, 1919
Prohibited the manufacture, sale, and transportation of intoxicating liquors
Third of four Progressive Era amendments; championed by the temperance movement (WCTU, Anti-Saloon League)
The only amendment to restrict individual rights — all others expand or protect them
Led to organized crime (Al Capone), speakeasies, and widespread flouting of the law
Repealed by the 21st Amendment (1933) — the only amendment to repeal another amendment
Prohibition lasted 13 years, 10 months, and 19 days

Ratified August 18, 1920
Prohibits denying the right to vote on account of sex
Fourth and final Progressive Era amendment; the women's suffrage movement had been active since the Seneca Falls Convention (1848)
The pivotal vote was cast by Harry Burn of Tennessee — the 36th state needed — persuaded by a note from his mother urging him to 'be a good boy'
Susan B. Anthony and Elizabeth Cady Stanton are the most famous suffragists; Anthony died 14 years before ratification
August 26 is Women's Equality Day, commemorating the certification of the 19th Amendment

items.answer: Lame Duck Amendment; presidential and congressional term start dates
items.explanation:
Ratified January 23, 1933
Moved presidential inauguration from March 4 to January 20; moved congressional sessions to begin January 3
Known as the 'Lame Duck Amendment' because it reduced the time between election and inauguration
Before this, 'lame duck' presidents and congressmen held power for four months after losing elections
Also addresses presidential succession if the president-elect dies before taking office
items.cardBack: Lame Duck Amendment; presidential and congressional term start dates

items.explanation:
Ratified December 5, 1933
Repeals the 18th Amendment, ending Prohibition
The only amendment to repeal another amendment in U.S. history
Uniquely ratified by state conventions rather than state legislatures — the only amendment to use this method
Roosevelt's election in 1932 signaled the end of Prohibition; the 21st Amendment was ratified less than a year into his presidency
Alcohol was legal again after nearly 14 years; however, many counties and municipalities remained 'dry' under local laws

Ratified February 27, 1951
Limits the president to two elected terms, or ten years total if completing more than two years of a predecessor's term
Passed after FDR won four consecutive terms (1932, 1936, 1940, 1944), breaking Washington's two-term precedent
Applies to all subsequent presidents; Eisenhower was the first president it applied to
Irony: passed by a Republican Congress but applies most notably to popular presidents like Eisenhower and Reagan, both Republicans
Does not prevent a former two-term president from serving as vice president

alternates: D.C. residents can vote for president
items.explanation:
Ratified March 29, 1961
Grants Washington D.C. the same number of electoral votes as the least-populous state (currently 3)
D.C. residents had been unable to vote in presidential elections since the capital was established in 1800
D.C. still has no voting representation in Congress (only a non-voting delegate in the House)
D.C. residents have voted overwhelmingly Democratic in every election since 1964; the margin is often 80–90%

items.answer: No poll taxes in federal elections
items.explanation:
Ratified January 23, 1964
Prohibits conditioning the right to vote in federal elections on payment of a poll tax or any other tax
Poll taxes were a key tool of Jim Crow disenfranchisement, particularly in Southern states
Harper v. Virginia (1966) extended this to state elections via the 14th Amendment's Equal Protection Clause
Part of the broader civil rights movement of the early 1960s; the Civil Rights Act of 1964 was passed the same year
items.cardBack: No poll taxes in federal elections

alternates: VP becomes president if president incapacitated
items.explanation:
Ratified February 10, 1967
Clarifies presidential succession: the VP becomes president if the president dies, resigns, or is removed; establishes procedures for presidential disability
Passed after Kennedy's assassination (1963) revealed gaps in succession law
Section 4 allows the VP and Cabinet to declare the president unable to discharge his duties — never invoked for its full purpose, though temporarily used during Reagan's surgery
Invoked twice under Nixon: when Ford was appointed VP (1973) and when Ford assumed the presidency (1974)
Section 2 filled the first peacetime VP vacancy (Nixon appointed Ford; Ford then appointed Rockefeller)

Ratified July 1, 1971
Lowers the voting age from 21 to 18 in all federal, state, and local elections
Passed largely in response to the Vietnam War — the argument was that if 18-year-olds could be drafted and die for their country, they should be able to vote
Oregon v. Mitchell (1970) had split the Court on whether Congress could lower the voting age for state elections, requiring a constitutional amendment
Added ~11 million new eligible voters when ratified; ratified in just 100 days — the fastest ratification of any amendment

items.answer: Congressional pay raises delayed until after an election
alternates: No immediate pay raises for Congress
items.explanation:
Ratified May 7, 1992
Provides that no law changing congressional compensation can take effect until after an intervening election
Originally proposed by James Madison in 1789 as part of the original Bill of Rights, but only 6 of the required 11 states ratified it at the time
Laid dormant for nearly 200 years until Gregory Watson, a University of Texas student, discovered it in 1982 and launched a ratification campaign
The 203-year gap between proposal (1789) and ratification (1992) is the longest in U.S. history
Watson received a C on his paper arguing the amendment could still be ratified; his professor later changed the grade to an A
items.cardBack: Congressional pay raises delayed until after an election

items.prompt: This war, fought 1775–1783, began with shots at Lexington and Concord, turned in America's favor at Saratoga when France entered as an ally, and ended with a British surrender at Yorktown. What war was this?
items.explanation:
Fought 1775–1783 between 13 American colonies and Great Britain
First shots fired at Lexington and Concord on April 19, 1775 — 'the shot heard round the world'
Battle of Saratoga (1777) was the turning point — France entered as an American ally, providing crucial naval support
George Washington crossed the Delaware River on Christmas night 1776 and won at Trenton
British surrendered at Yorktown, Virginia, on October 19, 1781
Ended with the Treaty of Paris (September 3, 1783) — Britain recognized American independence
Declaration of Independence issued July 4, 1776, during the conflict; inspired the French Revolution (1789)

items.prompt: During this conflict fought 1812–1815, British forces burned Washington D.C. and the White House, Francis Scott Key wrote 'The Star-Spangled Banner,' and Andrew Jackson became a hero at a battle fought after the peace treaty was already signed. What war was this?
items.explanation:
Fought 1812–1815 between the U.S. and Great Britain (and British Canada)
Causes included British impressment of American sailors, trade interference, and British support for Native American resistance
British burned Washington D.C., including the White House and Capitol, in August 1814
'The Star-Spangled Banner' written by Francis Scott Key during the bombardment of Fort McHenry, September 13–14, 1814
Battle of New Orleans (January 8, 1815) was fought after the Treaty of Ghent ended the war — news hadn't arrived; made Andrew Jackson a national hero
Ended with the Treaty of Ghent (December 24, 1814), restoring pre-war boundaries — effectively a draw
Sometimes called 'America's Second War of Independence'

items.prompt: This war fought 1846–1848 resulted in Mexico ceding roughly 525,000 square miles to the U.S. — including California, New Mexico, and Arizona — via the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo. Ulysses S. Grant and Robert E. Lee both served as officers. What war was this?
items.explanation:
Fought 1846–1848; triggered by U.S. annexation of Texas (1845) and a disputed border at the Rio Grande
U.S. forces under Generals Zachary Taylor and Winfield Scott captured Mexico City in September 1847
Ended with the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo (February 2, 1848): Mexico ceded ~525,000 sq miles including California, Nevada, Utah, Arizona, New Mexico, and parts of Colorado and Wyoming
U.S. paid Mexico $15 million in compensation for the ceded territory
Both Ulysses S. Grant and Robert E. Lee served as officers — future Civil War commanders on opposing sides
Led to fierce debate over slavery in new territories, catalyzing the sectional crisis that led to the Civil War
First U.S. war fought primarily on foreign soil; first American war to be photographed

items.prompt: This war fought 1861–1865 saw the bloodiest single day in American history at Antietam, the Emancipation Proclamation in 1863, and ended when Robert E. Lee surrendered to Ulysses S. Grant at Appomattox Court House. What war was this?
items.explanation:
Fought 1861–1865 between the Union (North) and the Confederate States of America (South)
Antietam (September 17, 1862) was the bloodiest single day in American history — ~23,000 casualties
Emancipation Proclamation issued January 1, 1863, freeing enslaved people in Confederate states
Battle of Gettysburg (July 1–3, 1863) was the turning point; Lincoln delivered the Gettysburg Address at its dedication
Lee surrendered to Grant at Appomattox Court House, Virginia, April 9, 1865
~620,000–750,000 soldiers died — more American deaths than in any other war; roughly 2–3% of the U.S. population
Lincoln was assassinated by John Wilkes Booth five days after Lee's surrender; Reconstruction Amendments (13th, 14th, 15th) followed

items.prompt: This 10-week war in 1898 began after the explosion of the USS Maine in Havana Harbor, saw Theodore Roosevelt charge up San Juan Hill with the Rough Riders, and resulted in the U.S. acquiring Puerto Rico, Guam, and the Philippines. What war was this?
items.explanation:
Lasted ~10 weeks (April–August 1898); Secretary of State John Hay called it 'a splendid little war'
Precipitated by the explosion of USS Maine in Havana Harbor (February 15, 1898) — 'Remember the Maine!'
Theodore Roosevelt led the Rough Riders cavalry charge at San Juan Hill and Kettle Hill in Cuba
U.S. won decisively: Spain ceded Cuba (independence), Puerto Rico, Guam, and the Philippines
Philippines acquired for $20 million; sparked the Philippine-American War (1899–1902) against Filipino independence fighters
Marked U.S. emergence as a global power with overseas territories; sometimes called the start of 'the American century'
William Randolph Hearst's 'yellow journalism' helped inflame public opinion toward war

items.prompt: The U.S. entered this war in April 1917 after the Zimmermann Telegram and unrestricted submarine warfare pushed Woodrow Wilson to act; the armistice was signed on the eleventh hour of the eleventh day of the eleventh month, 1918. What war was this?
items.explanation:
U.S. entered April 6, 1917 under President Woodrow Wilson; the overall war ran 1914–1918
U.S. entry triggered by German unrestricted submarine warfare (sinking of RMS Lusitania, 1915) and the Zimmermann Telegram
American Expeditionary Forces commanded by General John 'Black Jack' Pershing
Armistice signed November 11, 1918 at 11:00 AM — 'the eleventh hour of the eleventh day of the eleventh month'
~116,000 American deaths; ~20 million worldwide, plus ~50 million from the 1918 influenza pandemic
Treaty of Versailles (1919): harsh reparations on Germany; U.S. Senate rejected it and the League of Nations
Wilson's Fourteen Points and failure to ratify the Versailles Treaty foreshadowed the conditions leading to World War II

items.prompt: The U.S. entered this war after Japan attacked Pearl Harbor on December 7, 1941 — 'a date which will live in infamy'; it ended in Europe on V-E Day (May 8, 1945) and in the Pacific after atomic bombs were dropped on Hiroshima and Nagasaki. What war was this?
items.explanation:
U.S. entered after Japan attacked Pearl Harbor, Hawaii, December 7, 1941 — 'a date which will live in infamy' (FDR)
Allied strategy: 'Europe First' — defeat Nazi Germany before focusing on Japan
D-Day (June 6, 1944): Allied invasion at Normandy beaches — largest amphibious operation in history; ~156,000 troops landed
V-E Day (Victory in Europe): May 8, 1945 — Germany's unconditional surrender
Atomic bombs dropped on Hiroshima (August 6) and Nagasaki (August 9), 1945; Japan surrendered September 2, 1945 (V-J Day)
~405,000 American deaths; ~70–85 million total worldwide — the deadliest conflict in human history
Postwar: the United Nations (1945), Marshall Plan, NATO (1949), and the beginning of the Cold War

items.prompt: This conflict began when North Korea invaded South Korea on June 25, 1950; General MacArthur was fired by President Truman for publicly challenging civilian authority; it ended in a 1953 armistice restoring the boundary near the 38th parallel. What war was this?
items.explanation:
North Korea invaded South Korea on June 25, 1950; UN Security Council (with USSR absent) authorized intervention
General MacArthur's Inchon Landing (September 1950) recaptured Seoul in a bold amphibious operation
China entered October 1950 after UN forces pushed toward the Chinese border
MacArthur fired by Truman for publicly challenging civilian authority — a landmark moment for civil-military relations
Ended with the Korean Armistice Agreement (July 27, 1953) — boundary restored near the 38th parallel
~36,000 American deaths; ~3 million total Korean civilian and military deaths
Called 'The Forgotten War' — no formal peace treaty was ever signed; North and South Korea remain technically at war

items.prompt: This conflict escalated after the Gulf of Tonkin Resolution in 1964; at its peak in 1968, over 536,000 U.S. troops were deployed; the Tet Offensive devastated American public support; Saigon fell April 30, 1975. What war was this?
items.explanation:
Communist North Vietnam vs. U.S.-backed South Vietnam; Ho Chi Minh led the North
Gulf of Tonkin Resolution (August 1964) gave President Johnson broad war powers without a formal declaration
At peak (1968), ~536,000 U.S. troops deployed; 58,220 American deaths are listed on the Vietnam Veterans Memorial
Tet Offensive (January 1968): military failure for the North but devastated American public support for the war
My Lai Massacre (1968) and Pentagon Papers (1971) further eroded public trust; massive anti-war movement at home
U.S. withdrew under the Paris Peace Accords (January 27, 1973); Saigon fell April 30, 1975
First war the U.S. clearly lost; led to the War Powers Resolution (1973) limiting presidential war powers

items.prompt: This 1990–1991 conflict began when Saddam Hussein invaded Kuwait; Operation Desert Storm's 42-day air campaign was followed by a 100-hour ground war that liberated Kuwait but did not advance to Baghdad. What war was this?
items.explanation:
Iraq under Saddam Hussein invaded and annexed Kuwait on August 2, 1990
U.S.-led coalition of 34 nations, authorized by UN Security Council Resolution 678
Operation Desert Shield (August 1990–January 1991): buildup of ~700,000 coalition troops
Operation Desert Storm launched January 17, 1991: 42-day air campaign, then 100-hour ground war (February 24–28, 1991)
Kuwait liberated; President George H.W. Bush declared ceasefire; coalition did not advance to Baghdad or remove Saddam Hussein
~383 American combat deaths — remarkably low due to air superiority and swift ground campaign
First war covered live on television (CNN); Colin Powell and Norman Schwarzkopf became household names

items.prompt: Launched October 7, 2001, after the September 11 attacks; became the longest war in American history at 20 years; Osama bin Laden was killed by Navy SEALs in 2011; the Taliban retook Kabul in August 2021. What war was this?
items.explanation:
Launched October 7, 2001, after September 11 attacks; Taliban government had sheltered al-Qaeda leader Osama bin Laden
Taliban government fell quickly in November 2001; bin Laden escaped to Pakistan
Osama bin Laden killed by U.S. Navy SEALs in Abbottabad, Pakistan, on May 2, 2011
Longest war in American history: 20 years, spanning four presidencies (Bush, Obama, Trump, Biden)
Taliban retook Kabul on August 15, 2021, after U.S. withdrawal under agreements negotiated by the Trump administration
Chaotic final evacuation completed August 30, 2021; ISIS-K suicide bombing killed 13 U.S. service members at Kabul airport
~2,400 American military deaths; estimated total cost of $2.3 trillion

items.prompt: This war launched March 20, 2003, citing Iraqi weapons of mass destruction that were never found; Saddam Hussein was captured in a spider hole in December 2003; 'The Surge' in 2007 reduced violence; U.S. troops withdrew in 2011. What war was this?
items.explanation:
Launched March 20, 2003, by a U.S.-led coalition citing Iraqi WMDs and links to terrorism — no WMDs were found
Baghdad fell April 9, 2003; iconic image: Saddam Hussein's statue toppled in Firdos Square
Saddam Hussein captured in a spider hole near Tikrit on December 13, 2003; executed by hanging December 30, 2006
Abu Ghraib prisoner abuse scandal (2004) severely damaged U.S. credibility
'The Surge' (2007): 20,000 additional troops under General David Petraeus significantly reduced violence
U.S. combat troops withdrew December 18, 2011 under President Obama
~4,500 American deaths; 100,000–200,000+ Iraqi civilian deaths estimated; destabilization contributed to the rise of ISIS

items.prompt: In this 1803 case, Chief Justice John Marshall ruled that a law Marbury relied on was unconstitutional, establishing for the first time that the Supreme Court has authority to strike down laws that violate the Constitution. What case was this?
items.explanation:
Decided 1803 by Chief Justice John Marshall; arose from 'midnight appointments' by outgoing President Adams
William Marbury sued Secretary of State James Madison for withholding his commission as a justice of the peace
Marshall ruled the relevant section of the Judiciary Act of 1789 was unconstitutional
Established judicial review — the Supreme Court's authority to strike down laws that violate the Constitution
Marshall's masterstroke: ruled against Marbury on the immediate matter while vastly expanding the Court's power
Foundation of all subsequent constitutional law; has never been overturned

items.prompt: In this 1819 case, Chief Justice John Marshall held that Congress has 'implied powers' beyond its enumerated powers and that federal law is supreme over conflicting state law — specifically ruling Maryland could not tax the Second Bank of the United States. What case was this?
items.explanation:
Decided 1819 by Chief Justice John Marshall; Maryland taxed the Second Bank of the United States
Established 'implied powers': Congress may use any reasonable means to carry out its enumerated powers ('necessary and proper' clause)
'The power to tax is the power to destroy' — Marshall's famous phrase
Established federal supremacy over conflicting state laws under the Supremacy Clause
Foundation of modern federal regulatory power; cited in cases involving federal programs for centuries

items.prompt: In this 1857 case, Chief Justice Roger Taney ruled that Black Americans — enslaved or free — were not citizens, had no right to sue in federal court, and that Congress could not prohibit slavery in territories, declaring the Missouri Compromise unconstitutional. What case was this?
items.explanation:
Decided 1857 by Chief Justice Roger Taney; Dred Scott sought freedom after living in free states with his owner
Taney ruled Black Americans (enslaved or free) were not citizens and had no right to sue in federal court
Declared the Missouri Compromise of 1820 unconstitutional; Congress could not prohibit slavery in territories
Taney wrote Black people 'had no rights which the white man was bound to respect'
Widely considered the worst decision in Supreme Court history; accelerated tensions leading to the Civil War
Effectively overturned by the 13th Amendment (1865) and 14th Amendment (1868)

items.prompt: In this 1896 case, the Supreme Court upheld Louisiana's racial segregation of railway cars, establishing the 'separate but equal' doctrine that would provide constitutional cover for Jim Crow laws for nearly 60 years. Only Justice John Marshall Harlan dissented. What case was this?
items.explanation:
Decided 1896, 7–1; the lone dissent was by Justice John Marshall Harlan: 'our Constitution is color-blind'
Homer Plessy, a man of mixed race, deliberately challenged Louisiana's Separate Car Act to test the law
Established 'separate but equal' doctrine legalizing racial segregation in public facilities
Provided constitutional cover for Jim Crow laws throughout the South for nearly 60 years
Overturned by Brown v. Board of Education (1954)
Harlan's dissent predicted the ruling would be 'quite as pernicious' as Dred Scott

items.prompt: In this unanimous 1954 decision, Chief Justice Earl Warren declared that 'separate educational facilities are inherently unequal,' overturning Plessy v. Ferguson and ending the legal basis for school segregation. Thurgood Marshall argued for the NAACP. What case was this?
items.explanation:
Unanimous 1954 decision by Chief Justice Earl Warren; Thurgood Marshall argued for the NAACP
Consolidated five cases from Kansas, South Carolina, Virginia, Delaware, and Washington D.C.
'Separate educational facilities are inherently unequal' — Warren's opinion
Overturned Plessy v. Ferguson (1896), ending the legal basis for school segregation
Southern 'Massive Resistance': Arkansas Gov. Faubus blocked Little Rock's Central High (1957); Eisenhower sent federal troops
Thurgood Marshall later became the first Black Supreme Court Justice (1967)
Brown II (1955) ordered desegregation 'with all deliberate speed'

items.prompt: In this 1961 case, the Supreme Court applied the 4th Amendment's exclusionary rule to state courts, ruling that evidence obtained through unconstitutional searches is inadmissible — after Cleveland police searched Dollree Mapp's home without a warrant. What case was this?
items.explanation:
Decided 1961; Cleveland police searched Dollree Mapp's home without a warrant and found obscene materials
Applied the 4th Amendment's exclusionary rule to state courts via the 14th Amendment
'Fruit of the poisonous tree' — evidence obtained through illegal searches is inadmissible
Previously, the exclusionary rule only applied to federal courts (Weeks v. United States, 1914)
Part of the Warren Court's landmark expansion of defendants' rights in the 1960s

items.prompt: In this 1962 case, the Supreme Court ruled that a brief nondenominational prayer written by New York's Board of Regents for public schools violated the Establishment Clause, even though the prayer was voluntary. What case was this?
items.explanation:
Decided 1962; New York's Board of Regents had written a brief nondenominational prayer for public schools
Ruled that official state-sponsored prayer in public schools violates the Establishment Clause, even if voluntary
Justice Potter Stewart's lone dissent argued the ruling 'denies the existence of God'
One of the most controversial Warren Court decisions among religious Americans
Extended by *Abington School District v. Schempp* (1963), which banned Bible readings in public schools

items.prompt: In this 1966 case, the Supreme Court ruled that suspects must be informed of their right to remain silent and their right to an attorney before police interrogation, after Ernesto Miranda confessed to kidnapping and rape without being told of his rights. What case was this?
items.explanation:
Decided 1966, 5–4 by Chief Justice Earl Warren; Ernesto Miranda arrested in Phoenix, confessed without being informed of his rights
Established the 'Miranda warning': right to remain silent, right to an attorney, warning that statements can be used against you
Congress tried to overrule it (18 U.S.C. § 3501, 1968); the Court reaffirmed Miranda in Dickerson v. United States (2000)
Miranda himself was retried and convicted without his confession; he was stabbed to death in 1976
The phrase 'you have the right to remain silent' is now ubiquitous in American culture and television

items.prompt: In this unanimous 1967 case, Chief Justice Earl Warren struck down Virginia's law making interracial marriage a crime, ruling that 'marriage is one of the basic civil rights of man' and that anti-miscegenation laws violated the 14th Amendment. What case was this?
items.explanation:
Unanimous 1967 decision by Chief Justice Earl Warren
Richard Loving (white) and Mildred Jeter (Black and Native American) married in D.C. in 1958; Virginia sentenced them to 1 year, suspended if they left the state for 25 years
'Marriage is one of the basic civil rights of man' — Warren's opinion
Violated both the Equal Protection and Due Process Clauses of the 14th Amendment
June 12 is celebrated annually as 'Loving Day'
Cited as precedent in Obergefell v. Hodges (2015) legalizing same-sex marriage

items.prompt: In this 7–2 decision from 1973, Justice Harry Blackmun established a trimester framework for abortion rights, finding that Texas laws criminalizing abortion violated a constitutional right to privacy. It was overturned 49 years later in Dobbs v. Jackson. What case was this?
items.explanation:
7–2 decision in 1973, written by Justice Harry Blackmun; 'Jane Roe' (Norma McCorvey) challenged Texas laws criminalizing abortion
Established a trimester framework: no restrictions in 1st trimester; health regulations in 2nd; bans permitted in 3rd
Became one of the most contested decisions in American history; reshaped U.S. politics for 50 years
Reaffirmed by *Planned Parenthood v. Casey* (1992), which replaced the trimester framework with an 'undue burden' standard
Overturned by Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization on June 24, 2022 (6–3), returning abortion regulation to states
Norma McCorvey never had an abortion — the case took too long; she later became an anti-abortion activist

items.prompt: In this unanimous 1974 decision, the Supreme Court ruled that President Nixon could not withhold 64 Watergate tape recordings by claiming absolute executive privilege — leading directly to Nixon's resignation. What case was this?
items.explanation:
Unanimous 8–0 decision in 1974 (Rehnquist recused himself)
Nixon claimed absolute executive privilege to withhold 64 tape recordings subpoenaed by Watergate special prosecutor Leon Jaworski
Ruled executive privilege is not absolute when it conflicts with the judicial process in a criminal trial
Nixon released the tapes; the 'smoking gun' tape proved he directed the Watergate cover-up
Nixon announced his resignation August 8, 1974 — the only presidential resignation in U.S. history
Established that the President is not above the law

items.prompt: In this 5–4 decision issued December 12, 2000, the Supreme Court halted Florida's presidential recount, effectively awarding the state's 25 electoral votes — and the presidency — to George W. Bush over Al Gore. What case was this?
items.explanation:
5–4 per curiam decision, December 12, 2000; decided along partisan lines
Halted Florida's presidential recount, effectively giving George W. Bush Florida's 25 electoral votes
Held that varying recount standards across Florida counties violated the Equal Protection Clause
The majority opinion stated the ruling was limited to 'the present circumstances' — no precedential value
Bush won Florida by 537 votes out of ~6 million cast; he lost the national popular vote by ~543,000
Justice Stevens's dissent: 'the identity of the loser is perfectly clear. It is the Nation's confidence in the judge as an impartial guardian of the rule of law'
Most politically controversial Supreme Court decision of the modern era

items.prompt: In this 5–4 decision from 2008, Justice Antonin Scalia wrote the first Supreme Court opinion to directly hold that the 2nd Amendment protects an individual's right to keep a handgun at home for self-defense, striking down Washington D.C.'s handgun ban. What case was this?
items.explanation:
5–4 decision in 2008, written by Justice Antonin Scalia
Dick Heller challenged D.C.'s handgun ban and requirement that lawfully owned firearms be kept disassembled
First Supreme Court case to directly address whether the 2nd Amendment protects an individual (not just militia) right
Ruling was limited: upheld prohibitions on felons and mentally ill possessing firearms; did not address all gun regulations
McDonald v. City of Chicago (2010) extended Heller to state and local governments via the 14th Amendment

items.prompt: In this 5–4 ruling from 2010, the Supreme Court held that political spending by corporations, associations, and labor unions is protected First Amendment speech, overturning parts of the McCain-Feingold Act and leading to the rise of Super PACs. What case was this?
items.explanation:
5–4 decision in 2010, written by Justice Anthony Kennedy
Citizens United, a conservative nonprofit, wanted to air a film critical of Hillary Clinton near the 2008 primary
Held that political spending by corporations, associations, and labor unions is protected First Amendment speech
Overturned parts of the McCain-Feingold Act; led to the rise of 'Super PACs'
In his 2010 State of the Union, President Obama criticized the ruling in front of the seated Justices; Justice Alito mouthed 'not true'
Justice Stevens's 90-page dissent called the ruling 'a threat to democracy'

items.prompt: In this 5–4 decision from 2015, Justice Anthony Kennedy wrote that the 14th Amendment requires states to license and recognize same-sex marriages, with the famous line 'No union is more profound than marriage.' Jim Obergefell had fought to have his late husband's name on his death certificate. What case was this?
items.explanation:
5–4 decision in 2015, written by Justice Anthony Kennedy
Jim Obergefell challenged Ohio's refusal to recognize his same-sex marriage to John Arthur, who died of ALS before the ruling
Held that the 14th Amendment requires states to license and recognize same-sex marriages
Kennedy: 'No union is more profound than marriage' — widely read aloud at weddings
Chief Justice Roberts's dissent: 'Just who do we think we are?'
Built on Loving v. Virginia (1967) and United States v. Windsor (2013)
All four major LGBT rights rulings were decided on June 26: Romer (1996), Lawrence (2003), Windsor (2013), Obergefell (2015)

config.prompt: Name the capitals of all 50 U.S. states

items.explanation:
Capital of Alabama since statehood in 1819
Known as the 'Cradle of the Confederacy' — Jefferson Davis was inaugurated as Confederate President here in 1861
Rosa Parks refused to give up her seat on a Montgomery bus in 1955, sparking the Montgomery Bus Boycott

Capital of Alaska; unique distinction of being accessible only by boat or plane — no roads connect it to the rest of Alaska or to the lower 48
Named after gold prospector Joe Juneau, who discovered gold there in 1880
Sits at the base of the Juneau Icefield, one of the largest icefields in North America

Capital of Arizona; the most populous state capital in the U.S. with over 1.6 million people
Named after the mythological phoenix bird — built on the ruins of a former Hohokam settlement
Located in the Sonoran Desert; one of the hottest large cities in the world

Capital of Arkansas; named for a small rocky outcrop on the Arkansas River used as a navigation landmark
Site of the Little Rock Central High School crisis (1957): nine Black students integrated the school over the governor's objections; Eisenhower sent federal troops
Bill Clinton was governor here before becoming president

Capital of California, not Los Angeles or San Francisco — a common misconception
Became the capital in 1854; situated at the confluence of the Sacramento and American Rivers
Hub of the California Gold Rush (1848–1855); terminus of the First Transcontinental Railroad (1869)

Capital of Colorado; called the 'Mile High City' because its official elevation is exactly 5,280 feet (one mile) above sea level
Founded in 1858 during the Pike's Peak Gold Rush
The step on the 13th step of the State Capitol building is exactly one mile above sea level

Capital of Connecticut; one of the oldest cities in the U.S., founded in 1635
Known as the 'Insurance Capital of the World' — the insurance industry has been centered here since the early 19th century
Mark Twain lived in Hartford and wrote *The Adventures of Tom Sawyer* and *Adventures of Huckleberry Finn* there

Capital of Delaware; located in the center of the state, chosen as capital in 1777 to replace the coastal city of New Castle
Delaware was the first state to ratify the U.S. Constitution (December 7, 1787), earning the nickname 'The First State'
Dover Air Force Base is one of the largest military airlift facilities in the country

Capital of Florida; chosen in 1824 as a midpoint between the existing capitals of Pensacola and St. Augustine
Not Miami, Jacksonville, or Orlando — Tallahassee is far north in the Florida panhandle
Florida State University and Florida A&M University are both located there

Capital of Georgia; one of the few U.S. state capitals that is also the state's largest city
Burned by General Sherman during his 'March to the Sea' in 1864; rebuilt and became a major hub of the New South
Birthplace of Martin Luther King Jr. and headquarters of Coca-Cola and CNN

Capital of Hawaii; located on the island of Oahu
Home to Pearl Harbor, site of the Japanese surprise attack on December 7, 1941, that brought the U.S. into World War II
Birthplace of President Barack Obama (1961); the only U.S. state capital in the Pacific Ocean

Capital of Idaho; the name is thought to derive from a French or Shoshone word meaning 'wooded' or 'good river'
Located in the Treasure Valley along the Boise River; one of the fastest-growing cities in the U.S.
Nicknamed the 'City of Trees' for its extensive urban forest

Capital of Illinois, not Chicago — a very common misconception
Home of Abraham Lincoln, who practiced law and served in the state legislature here before the presidency
Lincoln's presidential library, museum, and tomb are all in Springfield

Capital of Indiana; one of the few U.S. state capitals that is also the state's largest city
Home of the Indianapolis 500, the world's largest single-day sporting event by attendance, held every Memorial Day weekend since 1911
Nicknamed 'the Crossroads of America' — eight U.S. highways converge there

Capital of Iowa; name derived from a French term, possibly meaning 'of the monks' after a Trappist settlement
Hosts the Iowa caucuses, traditionally the first major electoral event in presidential primary season
Major center for insurance companies; sometimes called the 'Hartford of the West'

Capital of Kansas, not Kansas City (which is actually in Missouri, and also straddles the Kansas border)
Home of the landmark *Brown v. Board of Education* (1954) case — the plaintiff Oliver Brown was from Topeka
Topeka and Kansas City are the largest cities in Kansas, but Kansas City KS is much smaller than Kansas City MO

Capital of Kentucky, not Louisville or Lexington — both of which are much larger cities
Chosen as the capital in 1792 because of its central location in a river valley
Kentucky's bourbon distilleries are concentrated near Frankfort along the Kentucky Bourbon Trail

Capital of Louisiana; name means 'red stick' in French, referring to a blood-red cypress post that marked a tribal boundary
Home of Louisiana State University (LSU)
Huey Long, the populist governor and senator, built the current State Capitol (1932) — the tallest in the U.S. at 450 feet — and was assassinated in it in 1935

Capital of Maine, not Portland (the state's largest city)
One of the smallest state capitals by population; located on the Kennebec River
Maine is the only state whose name is a single syllable

Capital of Maryland, not Baltimore (the state's largest city)
Known as the 'Sailing Capital of the U.S.' — home to the U.S. Naval Academy (founded 1845)
Was briefly the U.S. capital (1783–1784); Washington resigned his commission here and the Treaty of Paris was ratified here

Capital of Massachusetts and its largest city; one of the oldest cities in the U.S., founded in 1630
Cradle of the American Revolution: site of the Boston Massacre (1770), Boston Tea Party (1773), and the first battles at Lexington and Concord (1775)
Home of Harvard University (1636) — the oldest university in the U.S.

Capital of Michigan, not Detroit (the state's largest city and far more famous city)
Became the capital in 1847; chosen partly because Detroit was considered too exposed to Canadian attack
Michigan State University is located in neighboring East Lansing

Capital of Minnesota; forms the 'Twin Cities' metropolitan area with Minneapolis
Minneapolis and Saint Paul are often confused — Minneapolis is larger; Saint Paul is the capital
Saint Paul has one of the largest Hmong communities in the United States

Capital of Mississippi; named after President Andrew Jackson
Became the capital in 1822; was partially burned by Union General Sherman in 1863
Site of significant civil rights history — Medgar Evers, NAACP field secretary, was assassinated here in 1963

Capital of Missouri; named after Thomas Jefferson
Chosen as the capital in 1821 to be centrally located between the state's population centers
St. Louis and Kansas City are both far larger; Jefferson City is one of the smaller state capitals

Capital of Montana; founded in 1864 as 'Last Chance Gulch' during a gold rush
By the 1880s it was said to have more millionaires per capita than any other U.S. city, due to gold and silver mining
Renamed Helena after Last Chance Gulch was deemed insufficiently dignified for a city

Capital of Nebraska; named after President Abraham Lincoln when Nebraska became a state in 1867
Home of the University of Nebraska Cornhuskers; the football stadium's capacity on game day makes it the third-largest 'city' in Nebraska
Omaha is larger but is not the capital

Capital of Nevada; one of the least populous state capitals in the U.S.
Named after frontiersman Kit Carson; became the capital when Nevada was admitted to the Union in 1864
Las Vegas and Reno are far larger — Carson City's small population makes it a classic 'not the biggest city is the capital' example

Capital of New Hampshire, not Manchester (the state's largest city)
Home of the New Hampshire primary, historically the first primary election in the U.S. presidential race
Franklin Pierce, the 14th president, was born in New Hampshire; the state has sent disproportionate political influence through its early primary

Capital of New Jersey, not Newark or Jersey City (both of which are much larger)
Site of the pivotal Battle of Trenton (December 26, 1776) — Washington crossed the Delaware and surprised the Hessian garrison
Trenton's motto: 'Trenton Makes, The World Takes,' referencing its industrial history

Capital of New Mexico; the oldest state capital in the U.S., founded by the Spanish in 1610 — predating the Pilgrims by a decade
At 7,000 feet elevation, it is the highest-altitude state capital in the U.S.
Albuquerque is much larger but is not the capital; Santa Fe is world-renowned for its art scene and adobe architecture

Capital of New York, not New York City — one of the most common 'not the biggest city' capital misconceptions
One of the oldest continuously chartered cities in the U.S. (1686); settled by the Dutch as Fort Nassau/Fort Orange
Located on the Hudson River, 150 miles north of New York City

Capital of North Carolina; named after Sir Walter Raleigh, who sponsored the failed Roanoke Colony
Part of the 'Research Triangle' with Durham and Chapel Hill — home to NC State, Duke, and UNC
Charlotte is the state's largest city but is not the capital

Capital of North Dakota; named after German Chancellor Otto von Bismarck to attract German investment in the Northern Pacific Railroad
Fargo is larger and better known, but Bismarck is the capital
Sitting Bull was killed near Bismarck in 1890, and Fort Abraham Lincoln — where Custer departed on his final campaign — is nearby

Capital of Ohio and its largest city; named after Christopher Columbus
Home of Ohio State University, one of the largest universities in the U.S. by enrollment
Planned as a new capital city in 1812; Cleveland and Cincinnati are older but smaller in the modern era

Capital of Oklahoma; one of the rare state capitals that is also the state's largest city
Founded in a single day — April 22, 1889 — during the Land Rush, when 50,000 settlers staked claims simultaneously
Site of the Oklahoma City bombing (April 19, 1995) — the deadliest domestic terrorist attack in U.S. history at the time

Capital of Oregon, not Portland (the state's largest city)
Located in the Willamette Valley, historically the terminus of the Oregon Trail
Portland often overshadows Salem in name recognition, making this a classic trivia answer

Capital of Pennsylvania, not Philadelphia or Pittsburgh
Site of the Three Mile Island nuclear accident (March 28, 1979) — the worst nuclear accident in U.S. history, at a plant on an island in the Susquehanna River nearby
Named after John Harris Sr., who established a trading post there in the early 18th century

Capital of Rhode Island; founded by Roger Williams in 1636 after he was banished from Massachusetts Bay Colony for religious dissent
One of the first places in the Americas to guarantee freedom of religion
Home of Brown University (Ivy League) and the Rhode Island School of Design (RISD)

Capital of South Carolina; chosen in 1786 as a compromise between coastal and inland populations
Burned by General Sherman's Union forces in February 1865 during the Carolina Campaign
Home of the University of South Carolina; Charleston is the state's oldest and most historically significant city but is not the capital

Capital of South Dakota; the only state capital that does not share its name with a Metropolitan Statistical Area
Pronounced 'PEER' (not like the French name); named after Fort Pierre, a fur trading post
One of the smallest state capitals by population; Sioux Falls is much larger but is not the capital

Capital of Tennessee and its largest city; known as 'Music City' for its central role in country music
Home of the Grand Ole Opry, Vanderbilt University, and the Country Music Hall of Fame
President Andrew Jackson's plantation, The Hermitage, is located just outside Nashville

Capital of Texas; named after Stephen F. Austin, 'Father of Texas,' who colonized the area
Home of the University of Texas Longhorns; a major tech hub nicknamed 'Silicon Hills'
Houston and Dallas are far larger, but Austin has been the capital since Texas declared independence from Mexico in 1839

Capital of Utah; founded in 1847 by Mormon (LDS) pioneers led by Brigham Young, who reportedly said 'This is the place'
Headquarters of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints
Hosted the 2002 Winter Olympics; situated at the foot of the Wasatch Mountains with world-class ski resorts nearby

Capital of Vermont; the smallest state capital in the U.S. by population — approximately 8,000 people
The only state capital without a McDonald's
Spelled 'Montpelier' (one 'l'), unlike Montpellier, France (two 'l's); named after the French city

Capital of Virginia; served as the capital of the Confederate States of America during the Civil War (1861–1865)
Fell to Union forces on April 3, 1865, just six days before Lee's surrender at Appomattox
Was the colonial capital before Washington D.C. was established; Northern Virginia suburbs near D.C. are far more populous

Capital of Washington state, not Seattle (the state's largest city by far)
Located at the southern tip of Puget Sound; named after the Olympic Mountains visible to the northwest
Evergreen State College is located in Olympia; the state takes its name from President George Washington

Capital of West Virginia; not to be confused with Charleston, South Carolina
West Virginia separated from Virginia in 1863 and joined the Union during the Civil War
The Kanawha River runs through Charleston; the state is the only one formed by seceding from a Confederate state

Capital of Wisconsin; named after President James Madison
Home of the University of Wisconsin–Madison, a major research university; the campus sits on an isthmus between two lakes
Milwaukee is the state's largest city but is not the capital

Capital of Wyoming and its largest city; named after the Cheyenne people
Wyoming was the first territory and state to grant women the right to vote (1869), earning the nickname 'The Equality State'
Cheyenne Frontier Days, held every July, is one of the largest outdoor rodeos in the world
